action_space.name: Therapeutic Skills Development and Self-Application
action_space.description: Therapist systematically practices, monitors, and self-applies core CBT techniques—including self-monitoring, thought records, conceptualization diagrams, core belief work, and other interventions—to refine skills, increase empathy for patient obstacles, and identify areas for improvement before and during clinical practice.

goal.objective: Enhance therapist's competence, confidence, and accurate teaching of CBT by experiencing common obstacles and refining interventions through self-application and supervision.

success_levels.ideal: Therapist has direct self-experience with all major CBT tools, anticipates patient difficulties, and customizes guidance skillfully in session.
success_levels.acceptable: Therapist self-applies most core methods, notices key learning points, and addresses the majority of patient struggles effectively.
success_levels.unsuccessful: Therapist skips self-practice, remains unaware of technique pitfalls, and cannot effectively coach or problem-solve with patients.

goal.reward_hint: Therapist skill development through personal use increases credibility, teaching accuracy, and empathy, supporting robust patient outcomes.

states: Therapist is newly practicing or seeking to improve in CBT.
When preparing to implement a new technique/intervention with a patient.
When experiencing difficulty applying CBT methods with patients.
When needing to anticipate or empathize with patient barriers to homework and skill acquisition.

micro_actions.name: Therapist Self-Monitoring of Mood and Automatic Thoughts
micro_actions.description: Regularly track and record one’s own emotional state and automatic thoughts, particularly when facing clinical difficulties or introducing new skills to patients.
states: Whenever the therapist notices personal disengagement, anxiety, or self-doubt.
During supervision or skill development.
Before teaching the technique to patients.

micro_actions.name: Complete Thought Records or Core Belief Worksheets Personally
micro_actions.description: Fill in cognitive records (e.g., Thought Record, Core Belief Worksheet, Conceptualization Diagram) about the therapist’s own thoughts and beliefs, especially related to therapy practice, to identify obstacles and improve empathy with patients.
states: Prior to teaching these tools to patients.
When therapist encounters resistance or avoidance with these tools in patients.

micro_actions.name: Anticipate and Reflect on Patient Obstacles
micro_actions.description: Ask oneself what would make the intervention difficult for the therapist and proactively consider supports, adapted presentation, or troubleshooting for the patient.
states: When assigning homework or a new skill.
On noticing patient reluctance or poor follow-through.

micro_actions.name: Peer/Supervisor Review of Recorded Sessions
micro_actions.description: Engage in review (audio/video or transcript) with a supervisor or peer for feedback on adherence and skill, using standardized rating scales (e.g., Cognitive Therapy Rating Scale).
states: After obtaining patient consent for session recording.
Periodically throughout training or professional development.

micro_actions.name: Utilize Rating Scales for Session and Skill Evaluation
micro_actions.description: Apply and review CBT-specific rating tools (e.g., Cognitive Therapy Rating Scale) to assess session quality, fidelity to technique, and to identify areas for improvement.
states: After session recordings.
During supervision or self-assessment.

micro_actions.name: Engage in Continuing Education and Training
micro_actions.description: Participate in trainings, conferences, or supervision relevant to CBT; review books, manuals, and resources regularly to update practice.
states: Regularly as part of professional development.
When encountering new types of patient presentations.

action_space.name: Assessment, Formulation, and Structured Case Documentation
action_space.description: Comprehensively assess, formulate, and document the patient case—including history, complaints, formulation, plan, and outcomes—according to standardized CBT principles, enabling targeted intervention and progress tracking.

goal.objective: Establish a clear, standardized case formulation and plan to guide therapy and provide a baseline for supervision and outcome review.

success_levels.ideal: All key domains (history, symptoms, formulation, strengths, treatment plan, progress) are documented and updated, supporting individualized, evidence-based care.
success_levels.acceptable: Most essential domains are covered, formulation is sufficient for competent practice but may lack some detail.
success_levels.unsuccessful: Key domains are missing, documentation insufficient, impeding targeted treatment and supervision.

goal.reward_hint: Structured case writing and formulation prevent drift, support clear goal-setting, and enable effective supervision and outcome evaluation.

states: During intake or when beginning work with a new patient.
When a comprehensive overview is required (e.g., supervision, report-writing, or treatment transitions).
Periodically or after substantial clinical changes.

micro_actions.name: Standardized Case Write-Up
micro_actions.description: Document the patient’s history, problems, cognitive-behavioral formulation, and treatment plan using the standardized case format for record-keeping and supervision.
states: At the start of therapy.
At major treatment milestones.
When seeking supervision or presenting the case.

conversation_states.Description: Current level of demonstrated and self-rated skill proficiency in core cognitive behavioral therapy techniques, as monitored by self-reflection or formal rating scales.
Therapist’s confidence in applying and teaching core CBT interventions (e.g., self-monitoring, thought records, belief change), which predicts performance and patient engagement.
Completeness and clarity of session and case documentation, including use of structured case write-up, session ratings, and summaries.
Frequency and quality of the therapist’s participation in supervision, peer review, or professional training activities to support practice improvement.

nodes.description: The individual receiving cognitive behavior therapy.
The clinician providing cognitive behavior therapy, whose skills, reflections, and supervision affect outcomes.
Degree of actual and self-perceived competence in core CBT techniques, as developed and measured through practice and feedback.
Therapist’s practice and experience of using CBT methods on themselves to learn and anticipate patient barriers.
Ongoing activities including supervision, peer review, formal training, and continuing education.
Systematic, written documentation of assessment, formulation, goals, and plan, used for supervision and progress tracking.
Standardized tools (e.g., Cognitive Therapy Rating Scale) for rating therapist session structure, adherence, and skill.
A dynamic, collaborative outline specifying broad and session-specific goals, interventions, and problem prioritization tailored to the patient.
The set of defined symptoms and disorders guiding CBT adaptation and intervention selection.
Overall and session-by-session outcome objectives established by patient and therapist.
A collaboratively set list of topics and problems prioritized for discussion and intervention each session.
Detailed breakdown and analysis of problematic situations, automatic thoughts, emotions, behaviors, and beliefs.
Modifications to standard CBT methods to accommodate specific diagnoses, comorbidities, developmental, or cultural factors.
Any difficulty or stuck point—diagnosis, alliance, structure, socialization, automatic thoughts, or environment—that impedes progress and requires collaborative identification and remediation.
Quality of the collaborative working relationship, including trust, empathy, agreement on goals, and mutual effort.
Therapist's own automatic thoughts, reactions, technical skill, and use of supervision impacting treatment process.

cautions.description: Attempting to instruct, model, or problem-solve CBT methods with patients without first personally experiencing and troubleshooting the technique yourself.
cautions.risk: Reduces teaching credibility and accuracy, leads to oversight of common obstacles, and impedes patient buy-in and adherence.

cautions.negative_action: Skipping Comprehensive Case Formulation or Documentation
cautions.description: Failing to complete a thorough written case history, formulation, and ongoing documentation for each patient.
cautions.risk: Leads to unfocused therapy, poor supervision utility, missed problems, and suboptimal outcomes.

cautions.negative_action: Practicing in Isolation Without Ongoing Supervision or Feedback
cautions.description: Avoiding peer/supervisor review, not recording sessions, or neglecting to use session/skill rating scales.
cautions.risk: Stagnant skill development, increased risk of blind spots or drift from standard practice, and reduced effectiveness.

cautions.negative_action: Neglecting to Tailor Techniques/Assignments for Patient Readiness and Case Complexity
cautions.description: Selecting challenging patients or complex cases for initial CBT without sufficient experience, or failing to adapt standard methods to fit patient characteristics.
cautions.risk: Increases likelihood of therapy failures, discourages both patient and therapist, and undermines fidelity to model.

cautions.negative_action: Reverting to Prior Non-CBT Modalities Without Patient Knowledge or Agreement
cautions.description: Unilaterally changing therapeutic approach due to therapist comfort, without rationalizing or discussing with the patient.
cautions.risk: Breaks collaborative empiricism, confuses patient, and hinders CBT progress.

categorical_dimensions.description: Spans emotional, anxiety, depressive, trauma-related, somatic, behavioral, comorbid, and culturally-shaped syndromes relevant to CBT/UP, reflecting functional impairment even when formal diagnostic criteria are unmet. Includes commonly comorbid and transdiagnostic presentations: anxiety and depressive disorders, trauma, compulsivity, chronic pain, sleep disturbance, eating/body image issues, substance use, non-suicidal and suicidal self-injury, ADHD, personality and cross-cultural/atypical syndromes (e.g., Taijin kyofusho), and complex cases with secondary or functionally impairing symptoms. Also acknowledges necessity of profile- and dimensional-based formulations (e.g., using MEDI), as well as readiness to identify features beyond categorical diagnosis.
options: categorical DSM diagnosis not clearly specified

categorical_dimensions.description: Incorporates individual, group, and stepped/multidisciplinary approaches, as well as flexible, modular, technology-facilitated, culturally tailored, and self-help options. Emphasizes the value of adaptation for cultural/functional needs, with group-based learning, cross-cultural delivery (including adaptation for stigma, language, rapport, case formulation), and task-shared/non-specialist implementation.
options: culturally adapted protocol (surface or deep level)

categorical_dimensions.description: Strengthens emphasis on the Unified Protocol’s transdiagnostic focus, rigorous outcome measurement (with standard tools/interviews), modular/personalized intervention, stepped care, group-based and culturally adapted options, and fidelity and implementation/dissemination models. Explicitly includes train-the-trainer and certification protocols, and a growing evidence base for adaptation/dissemination in non-specialist and international contexts.

Highlights high neuroticism/negative affect, emotion dysregulation, avoidance, and cognitive inflexibility as central, while also integrating traits including low extraversion/positive affect, anxiety sensitivity, experiential/behavioral avoidance, emotional suppression, perfectionism, hypervigilance, distress about sleep/body, shame, organizational/executive dysfunction, and skill/strengths deficits. Cross-cultural and syndromal heterogeneity is incorporated, as is functional impairment across domains.

free_form_dimensions.description: Patterns of emotional experience, regulation, and expression shaped by cultural context and personal history.
free_form_dimensions.value: Emotion regulation difficulties span avoidance, suppression, non-acceptance, rumination, impulsivity (self-injury, substance use, eating, sleep disturbance), and low emotional awareness/alexithymia. Many also exhibit variable capacity for mindful emotion awareness and cognitive flexibility. Regulation is further complicated by comorbidity, functional impairment, and contextual/cultural themes. Therapy builds adaptive, flexible, and accepting skills, with an emphasis on experiential and cognitive approaches and hierarchy-based exposures.

free_form_dimensions.description: Impact of co-occurring disorders and culturally/trauma-influenced presentations in cross-cultural settings.
free_form_dimensions.value: Comorbidity—including anxiety, depressive, trauma, substance, eating, sleep/pain, neurological (ADHD), and personality syndromes—is typical and guides transdiagnostic case conceptualization. Subclinical or functionally impairing syndromes and cultural syndromes (Taijin kyofusho, culturally-specific avoidance/shame presentations) are identified through broad, dimensional assessment. Cultural, ethnic, and context variables frequently interact with primary and secondary symptom expression.

free_form_dimensions.description: How culture, worldview, and identity shape symptom presentation, therapeutic rapport, and adaptation needs.
free_form_dimensions.value: Culture, ethnicity, and social context strongly influence symptom language, meaning, and presentation (e.g., somatic, self-harm, or avoidance expressed in culture-bound idioms), as well as rapport, trust, stigma, and adaptation needs. Culturally adapted assessment (e.g., the CFI/MEIM), translation, flexibility in process/delivery, and consideration of norms and barriers (including stigma and low access) are expected. Collaboration around adaptation level and specific needs is critical to engagement and effectiveness.

free_form_dimensions.description: How patients' cultural and personal narratives shape their view of emotions, self, and therapy.
free_form_dimensions.value: Emotional experiences may be judged as dangerous, shameful, or confusing, with themes of blame, loss, or perfection. Some patients lack emotional language or use somatic metaphors. Cultural narratives affect interpretation and coping. Therapy provides psychoeducation and exercises (ARC, mindful emotion awareness) to normalize emotions and address maladaptive beliefs, with attention to culture/language in practice and conceptualization.

free_form_dimensions.description: Most common emotion-driven and safety-seeking patterns, including culture- and trauma-specific expressions.
free_form_dimensions.value: Avoidance behaviors are extensive—encompassing cognitive (worry, suppression, rumination, distraction), behavioral (compulsions, substance use, self-injury, avoidance/social withdrawal), and somatic (pain, sleep, eating, safety signals) strategies. These are shaped by culture, family, and symptom subtype, and frequently reinforced by functional/contextual limitations. Therapy routinely incorporates identification and targeted intervention (emotion exposure hierarchies, cognitive tools) for avoidance, with personalization for cultural and contextual norms.

free_form_dimensions.description: Willingness and barriers to collaborative assessment and formulation, with a focus on cultural tailoring.
free_form_dimensions.value: Patients typically engage well when rationale for assessment is clear, tools are brief/adapted for language and literacy, and progress can be observed. Ethnic/cultural adaptations (CFI, MEIM) and team/tailored protocols support buy-in, especially for complex, functionally impaired, or marginalized populations. Willingness is enhanced by respect for culture, reduced stigma, and scheduled feedback on progress/accomplishment charts.

free_form_dimensions.description: How UP modules and process address culture- and trauma-linked mechanisms across diverse cases.
free_form_dimensions.value: All UP modules target transdiagnostic mechanisms—emotion avoidance, cognitive inflexibility, safety/avoidant behavior—across cultural and syndromal lines. Hierarchical and modular sequencing permits focus on dominant process (e.g., emotion-driven behaviors, mindfulness, interoceptive awareness) as relevant. Comorbidity and cultural overlap are expected, and therapy integrates flexible module selection and adaptation for culture, age, and comorbidity, including non-DSM problem constellations.

free_form_dimensions.description: Receptivity and obstacles to culturally relevant psychoeducation, rationale, and normalization.
free_form_dimensions.value: Psychoeducation is widely accepted when linked to culturally and personally relevant examples/goals and delivered at the right literacy/learning level, with translation and adaptation as needed. The importance of transdiagnostic models, normalization, and evidence for broad application is reinforced, including discussion of atypical, somatic, or culture-bound presentations and functional syndromes.

free_form_dimensions.dimension_name: Expectation Regarding Treatment Course and Adaptation
free_form_dimensions.description: Culturally and contextually anchored expectations regarding progress, engagement, and adaptation.
free_form_dimensions.value: Diversity of expectation is the norm: most anticipate adaptation of protocol (modality, length, modules, culturally relevant rapport-building or trust-enhancement, session translation/adjustment). Markers of quality—visible progress, skill generalization, culturally meaningful adaptation, flexible sequencing, and opportunity for relapse prevention and maintenance—are prioritized. Technology or non-specialist/organizational implementation is welcome when access/trust issues exist.

free_form_dimensions.description: Cultural and individual influences on acceptance of research-based interventions.
free_form_dimensions.value: Openness generally increases with transparency of rationale, involvement in monitoring, and validation from clinicians/institutions (including demonstration of cultural fit and safety). Skepticism (due to culture, prior experience, stigma, language) requires additional engagement and psychoeducation, and messaging about protocol adaptation and successful global/cultural dissemination further supports acceptance.

free_form_dimensions.description: Values for therapist qualities, structure, and culturally responsive adaptations.
free_form_dimensions.value: High value is placed on therapist flexibility, skill, and cultural responsiveness, as well as systematic adaptation (modality, content, process) to personal/contextual circumstance. Experience with group-based and multidisciplinary settings is valued, as is access to evidence-informed protocols, outcome monitoring, and personalized options for session length, frequency, pacing, and follow-up/relapse prevention.

free_form_dimensions.dimension_name: Receptivity to Multi-Disciplinary and Experienced Clinician Input
free_form_dimensions.description: Openness to integrating multiple professional recommendations and culturally tailored input.
free_form_dimensions.value: Patients are receptive to stepped, multidisciplinary, and team-delivered care when it improves access, cultural fit, and treatment consistency. Recognition of expertise (certification, implementation sites, trainer models) increases trust, and integration of psychiatric/medical/cultural perspectives (as part of shared case formulation/adaptation) is expected for complex/comorbid cases, especially when cultural/linguistic or medical adaptation is relevant.
